facts_disclosed: Considering moving to Dubai for design scene

turns.content: so hypothetical question. if you were an architect and you wanted a more experimental design scene but didn't want to move to a completely different country, where would you go in the Gulf?

Interesting question! Within the Gulf, Dubai would be the obvious answer for experimental architecture. It has a concentration of international firms doing ambitious work — OMA, Foster + Partners, Zaha Hadid Architects all have significant presences there. The scale and ambition of projects there tends to attract designers who want to push boundaries.

Riyadh is also emerging fast with the NEOM and Vision 2030 projects, though the scene there is newer. Doha has the museum and cultural district. But for sheer density of architectural experimentation and an established design community, Dubai leads.

Why the hypothetical? Are you weighing options?

okay fine it's not hypothetical lol. I've been looking at studios in Dubai tbh. like actually going on their websites and reading about their work and imagining myself there. the design scene is just more experimental than Abu Dhabi? here it's all masterplanned developments and government buildings and luxury residential. which is fine I'm learning a lot but I want to do weirder stuff you know

That makes a lot of sense. Abu Dhabi's architecture tends to be more institutional and planned — big cultural projects like Saadiyat Island, government commissions, high-end residential. Dubai has more of the speculative, developer-driven, boundary-pushing work. Pavilions, pop-ups, mixed-use experiments, competitions that actually get built.

What kind of studios are you looking at? Smaller experimental ones, or bigger firms with more resources?

a mix! there's this one called X Architects that does really thoughtful contextual work and they won the Aga Khan Award. and then there are smaller studios doing parametric design and digital fabrication. I mean I know Dubai has a reputation for like, putting a ski slope inside a mall, but there's actually serious design culture underneath the flashy stuff

X Architects is an excellent pick — their work bridges exactly what you seem to be looking for, combining Gulf context with contemporary experimentation. The Wasit Wetland Centre is a beautiful example of that.

And you're right about Dubai's depth beneath the surface. The flashy projects get the attention, but there's a whole layer of studios doing rigorous, research-driven work. Dubai Design District (d3) has also created a genuine community of designers, architects, and creatives that feeds collaboration.

Have you thought about what the move would actually look like practically? Is it just a job change, or would you be relocating your whole life?

that's the thing. it's only like an hour and a half drive from Abu Dhabi so it's not like moving to another country. but I'd need to actually live there because commuting every day would kill me. and living away from my parents would be... I mean it would force me to grow up? like actually cook and do my own laundry and figure out life without mama doing everything lol

There's something appealing about that kind of forced independence, especially when it comes with a safety net. You're close enough to drive home for Friday lunch if you want to, but far enough that you have to build your own daily life.

It's also worth considering the financial side. Dubai is significantly more expensive than Abu Dhabi for rent and daily living. A junior architect's salary there might not stretch as far. Have you looked at what studios are offering?

not in detail yet. I know rent in Dubai is insane rn, like sharing a 2BR apartment would still be maybe 3-4k dirhams a month for my share. Abu Dhabi I'm living at home so my rent is literally zero. wallah mama's cooking is worth like 5000 dirhams a month on its own tbh. financially it makes no sense

Ha! The economic value of a parent's cooking is genuinely underrated in career planning. But you're identifying the real tradeoff: Abu Dhabi gives you financial comfort and family proximity at the cost of the professional environment you're craving. Dubai gives you the design scene at the cost of financial pressure and distance from home.

Is there a middle path? Some people test the waters by applying to a few studios, seeing what offers come in, and letting the specifics of the opportunity tip the decision. You don't have to decide "Dubai or not" in the abstract — you could decide based on whether a specific studio excites you enough to justify the move.

that's true. I don't know. I'm just thinking out loud honestly. like part of me knows Abu Dhabi is the smart choice — cheaper, my family is here, my job is fine. but the other part of me is scrolling through X Architects' portfolio at midnight going I COULD BE DOING THIS

That midnight portfolio scrolling is telling you something. "Fine" and "I could be doing THIS" are very different levels of engagement with your career. The fact that you keep coming back to it suggests it's more than a passing thought.

Here's a low-stakes next step: reach out to people who've made the Abu Dhabi-to-Dubai move. LinkedIn, architecture forums, AUS alumni — someone in your network has done exactly this transition. Their experience with the financial reality, the lifestyle shift, and the professional difference would give you data instead of speculation.

You don't have to commit to anything. Just gather information.

okay yeah that's smart. I actually know someone from AUS who works at a studio in d3, I could message her. ugh I just need to stop going back and forth in my head and actually talk to real people about it. yalla thanks for entertaining my Dubai daydream lol

It's more than a daydream if you're researching studios and know someone who's already there! Message your AUS contact — a real conversation will do more than another month of hypotheticals.

And for what it's worth, both options have genuine merit. This isn't a case of a right choice and a wrong choice. It's two different versions of a good life. You'll figure out which one fits.

Good luck with the research!
